ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'tomato soup' entry (FOODON_03315595); Tomato Shorba is a spiced Indian variant — closest_parent match used as no exact Indian shorba entry exists in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GG covers soups and broths broadly; no specific Indian shorba code exists. closest_parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Tomato Shorba or Indian spiced tomato soup as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single serving of Tomato Shorba is approximately 200 ml (200 g), typically served as a starter or side soup in a small bowl or cup.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Tomato Shorba is a traditional Indian preparation. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods using tomato-based soup and spiced broth composition data. No exact Tomato Shorba entry exists in ICMR-NIN; values represent a close adaptation from tomato soup and related entries. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Tomato soup, home-prepared, USDA #06164) used as secondary cross-check for macro plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tomato Shorba. GI estimated at 25 (low) based on analogy with tomato soup and vegetable broth entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). Tomato Shorba has very low carbohydrate density (~5 g/100 g), predominantly from tomatoes with low starch content, supporting a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (25 × 5.0 × 2) / 100 = 2.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (180 mg/100 g); potassium-rich tomatoes support blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: Tomato Shorba contains 180 mg sodium per 100 g (360 mg per 200 ml serving), which is moderate; individuals with hypertension should limit added salt during preparation and can benefit from the potassium (220 mg/100 g) and lycopene content of tomatoes, which support vascular health.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI (~25) and low carbohydrate density (5 g/100 g) with minimal glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tomato Shorba has an estimated GI of approximately 25 and only 5 g of carbohydrate per 100 g, resulting in a glycemic load of approximately 2.5 per standard 200 ml serving, making it a suitable low-glycemic starter or side dish for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, lycopene-rich tomatoes, and potassium content support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tomato Shorba is low in saturated fat (0.3 g/100 g) and total fat (1.5 g/100 g), and the lycopene and potassium from tomatoes are associated with reduced cardiovascular risk; however, sodium content should be monitored and salt minimised in preparation for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Tomatoes are nightshade vegetables; some individuals with rheumatoid arthritis report sensitivity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tomatoes contain antioxidants including lycopene and vitamin C that may support anti-inflammatory pathways; however, individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables (Solanaceae family) should monitor their personal response and limit intake if symptoms worsen.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tomato Shorba is a traditional North Indian spiced tomato soup or broth, prepared by simmering ripe tomatoes with aromatic spices including cumin, ginger, garlic, black pepper, and coriander, finished with a tempering (tadka) of whole spices in oil or ghee. Unlike Western tomato soups, Tomato Shorba is characteristically thinner in consistency, more intensely spiced, and lower in cream or dairy content, though some regional variants add a small amount of gram flour (besan) for slight body or cream for richness. The flavour profile is tangy, warming, and mildly spicy, with a bright tomato-forward taste balanced by earthy cumin and the heat of ginger and pepper. Nutritionally, Tomato Shorba is a low-calorie food at approximately 35 kcal per 100 g, with low fat (1.5 g), moderate carbohydrate (5 g), and modest protein (0.8 g). It is a good source of vitamin C (12 mg/100 g), vitamin A (45 mcg RAE/100 g), and potassium (220 mg/100 g), and contains lycopene from cooked tomatoes. Its estimated glycemic index of approximately 25 makes it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic conditions. Tomato Shorba is commonly served as a starter or light side dish at lunch or dinner in North Indian households and restaurants, and is particularly popular during winter months as a warming comfort food. It is naturally vegetarian and can be made vegan by substituting ghee with oil.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Tomato Shorba?
Is Tomato Shorba good for diabetes?
What is the difference between Tomato Shorba and tomato soup?
What spices are used in Tomato Shorba?
Can I have Tomato Shorba if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact Tomato Shorba entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN tomato-based soup and broth data with USDA SR Legacy cross-check. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for tomato soup and vegetable broth. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (tomato soup / spiced broth entries) and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (USDA #06164, tomato soup home-prepared). No exact Tomato Shorba entry exists in any approved primary source. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: confirmed zero as this is a plant-based vegetarian preparation with no animal-derived ingredients in the standard recipe; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central for tomato-based vegetable soups. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero for this plant-based preparation with no UV-exposed mushrooms or fortified ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for comparable tomato soups. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant-based preparation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. trans_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero for home-prepared tomato broth without hydrogenated fats. 3. water_g set to -1: detailed moisture data for Tomato Shorba not available as a discrete entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central; estimated water content would be approximately 88–92 g/100 g for a thin broth but not confirmed by an approved source entry for this specific preparation. 4. Saturated fat (0.3 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.7 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.3 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable tomato soup with small amounts of vegetable oil or ghee; these are approximate values. 5. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for tomato soup and vegetable broth; no direct GI measurement for Tomato Shorba exists in the published literature. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (25 × 5.0 × 2) / 100 = 2.5. 6. Portions in input had three identical duplicate entries (200 ml, 200 g); deduplicated to one standard serving. 7. The dietary tag 'low_sodium' in the input is borderline — 180 mg/100 g yields 360 mg per 200 ml serving, which is moderate rather than low sodium; this tag has been noted but not carried forward as a dietary flag. 8. FoodOn match FOODON_03315595 (tomato soup) is a closest_parent rather than exact match; no specific Indian shorba ontology entry exists in FoodOn as of 2024. FoodEx2 A00GG (soups and broths) is a closest_parent match.
